title: Predicting the conditions for observing the Mpemba effect
authors: Yue Liu, Tan Van Vu, Raphaël Chétrite, Frédéric van Wijland, Hisao Hayakawa
abstract: The Mpemba effect, a counterintuitive phenomenon where a hotter system relaxes faster than a colder one, has been widely observed in various nonequilibrium systems. Despite this progress, the fundamental structural features of the energy landscape required for its emergence remain a subject of debate. In this study, we investigate the conditions for the Mpemba effect within one-dimensional overdamped Langevin dynamics. We classify the potential landscapes based on the presence of single or double wells, their symmetry properties, and the existence of walls. We establish that the existence of the effect is primarily driven by the presence of boundaries, either hard or soft, rather than the specific internal structure of the potential landscape, such as metastability or the number of minima. By employing a spectral decomposition of the Fokker-Planck operator, we analyze the behavior of the first nontrivial eigenmode and demonstrate that its derivative acts as a Dirac delta peak in the low-temperature regime. This helps us elucidate the mechanism underlying the Mpemba effect: it appears as the interplay between this behavior and the initial population dynamics in a non-trivial way induced by the presence of the wall. Our analysis provides a unified classification across single- and double-well potentials, highlighting the crucial role of boundary conditions and asymmetry. Furthermore, we demonstrate that this framework allows for the engineering of potential landscapes capable of producing multistage Mpemba transitions.
summary: This theoretical study predicts the necessary structural conditions for observing the Mpemba effect in overdamped Langevin systems. It shows that the effect is fundamentally governed by the presence of boundaries (walls) rather than the internal details of the potential wells. The analysis uses spectral methods to classify potentials and engineer systems exhibiting this counterintuitive thermal relaxation behavior.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental structural features of the energy landscape required for the Mpemba effect, a counterintuitive phenomenon where a hotter system relaxes faster than a colder one.
main_result: The existence of the Mpemba effect is primarily driven by the presence of boundaries (hard or soft walls), rather than the specific internal structure of the potential landscape. The analysis provides a unified classification across single- and double-well potentials.
method: The study employs spectral decomposition of the Fokker-Planck operator and analyzes the behavior of the first nontrivial eigenmode, relating the effect to the non-monotonic dependence of the overlap coefficient on initial temperature.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using one-dimensional overdamped Langevin dynamics for a particle in a potential V(x), governed by the Fokker-Planck equation. The analysis covers single-well and double-well potentials, considering the influence of hard or soft boundaries.
key_observables: Overlap coefficient ($a_2$), Mpemba temperature ($ eta_M$), and the behavior of the first nontrivial eigenmode.
important_parameters: Initial temperature ($T_i$) or inverse temperature ($ eta_i$), and the presence/location of boundaries ($L_-, L_+$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on asymptotic approximations, particularly in the low-bath-temperature regime, and assumes the potential grows fast enough at the boundaries.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate decision trees summarizing conditions for the Mpemba effect, and plots show the dependence of $ eta_M$ on wall separation for various potential types.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the mathematical framework using the Fokker-Planck operator, deriving the condition for the Mpemba effect via the overlap coefficient $a_2$, and finally classifying the necessary structural elements (boundaries) required for the effect to manifest.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, mathematically grounded classification of the Mpemba effect, linking it directly to boundary conditions and spectral properties of the underlying dynamics, which is highly relevant for understanding non-equilibrium relaxation in complex systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Summary of the conditions for observing the Mpemba effect across various potential landscapes. The decision tree categorizes one-dimensional potentials based on the number of wells (single well vs. double well), their parity (symmetrical vs. asymmetrical), and the boundary conditions (without wall vs. with wall(s)). The green checkmark and red cross denote the guaranteed presence and absolute absence of the Mpemba effect, respectively. Simultaneous checkmark and cross mean that the existence of the effect is conditionally dependent on the position of the wall(s).

FIG. 2. (a) A symmetric single-well potential is equivalent to an asymmetric single-well potential with a wall at the center. (b) The symmetric double-well potential is equivalent to an asymmetric single-well potential with a wall at the center of the symmetric potential.

FIG. 3. (a) The inverse temperature βM is shown as a function of L−for various bath temperatures in a quartic po- tential V (x) ∼x4 with a hard wall at x = −L−= −LBL. (b) Same as in which the quartic potential is replaced by a sextic potential V (x) ∼x6. In both (a) and (b), the analyt- ical result, Eq. (45), obtained for large β and L−is shown as a dashed line as a guide for the eye. Remarkably, no no- table deviations are observed, even very far from the regime in which the analytical expression was derived. We have used L+ = 2L−(LBR = 2LBL).

FIG. 4. Plot of the Mpemba inverse temperature βM as a function of the position L−of the soft wall is shown for two different simulation boxes LBL = 300 (red solid triangles) and 500 (blue open circles), and the result is independent of LBL. The dashed line shows excellent agreement with our prediction. We have used m = 4, a bath temperature β = 1, and the soft soft wall grows as V (x) ∼x6 (q = 6) for −LBR > x > −L−. Here, we have used L+ = LBR = 2LBL.

FIG. 5. (a) The overlap coefficient a3 as a function of βi for the symmetric double-well potential constructed by a piece- wise harmonic potential, which exhibits a non-monotonic be- havior, indicating the existence of the (inverse) Mpemba ef- fect. The analytical result (black solid line) is consistent with the numerical result (red dashed line). (b) The time evolution of the KL divergence for cold initial temperature (βi = 20, blue solid line) and warm initial temperature (βi = 8, orange solid line), which cross each other, thereby confirming the (in- verse) Mpemba effect.
